source_paper_title: Square-Root Regret Bounds for Continuous-Time Episodic Markov Decision Processes
context:
Page 14, Section 4 (Main Results), discussion immediately following Theorem 2

"While the bounds on K are the most important as they inform the convergence rates of learning algorithms, it remains a significant open question whether one can improve the dependence of these bounds on S, H and λmax to narrow down the gap between the upper and lower bounds. ... Azar et al. (2017) consider Bernstein-type “exploration bonuses” that use the empirical variance of the estimated value function at the next state to achieve tight (linear) dependance on H (in the leading term of their regret bound). It is an problem how to extend their idea and technique to our setting in order to improve the dependancy of the upper bound on H."
title: Bernstein-style regret bound for episodic CTMDPs with improved dependence on horizon and state size
latex:
Consider a finite-horizon episodic continuous-time Markov decision process (CTMDP) with finite state space \(\mathcal S\) (\(|\mathcal S|=S\)), finite action space \(\mathcal A\) (\(|\mathcal A|=A\)), horizon \([0,H]\), known reward rate \(r:\mathcal S\times\mathcal A\to[0,1]\), unknown holding-time rates \(\lambda:\mathcal S\times\mathcal A\to[\lambda_{\min},\lambda_{\max}]\) with known \(\lambda_{\max}\), and unknown transition probabilities \(p(\cdot\mid x,a)\) on \(\mathcal S\). Under a (deterministic Markov) policy \(\pi\), the process in state \(x\) taking action \(a\) accrues reward at rate \(r(x,a)\) for an exponential holding time \(\tau\sim\mathrm{Exp}(\lambda(x,a))\) (truncated at the episode end), then jumps to \(Y\sim p(\cdot\mid x,a)\).

An episodic learning algorithm interacts with an unknown CTMDP instance \(M\) for \(K\) episodes, each restarting from a fixed initial state \(x_0\). Let \(V^*(x_0,H)\) be the optimal expected cumulative reward over \([0,H]\) when \(M\) is known, and let \(V^{\pi_k}(x_0,H)\) be the expected cumulative reward in episode \(k\) under the algorithm’s chosen policy \(\pi_k\). The expected regret after \(K\) episodes is
\[
\mathrm{Regret}(M,\mathrm{algo},K)=\mathbb E\Big[\sum_{k=1}^K \big(V^*(x_0,H)-V^{\pi_k}(x_0,H)\big)\Big].
\]
Let \(\mathcal C\) denote the class of such CTMDPs satisfying the bounds above.

Problem: Design an episodic reinforcement learning algorithm for this CTMDP class \(\mathcal C\) that achieves a worst-case (minimax) expected regret upper bound with improved dependence on \(H\) (and ideally on \(S\) and \(\lambda_{\max}\)) compared to the CT-UCBVI bound in Theorem 1 of the paper, by extending Bernstein-type (empirical-variance) exploration bonuses and accompanying analysis from discrete-time tabular MDPs (e.g., Azar et al., 2017) to the continuous-time episodic setting with random, unbounded numbers of transitions per episode. Formally, prove an upper bound of the form
\[
\sup_{M\in\mathcal C} \mathrm{Regret}(M,\mathrm{algo},K) \le \tilde O\big(f(S,A,H,\lambda_{\min},\lambda_{\max})\,\sqrt{K}\big)
\]
where \(f\) improves the current worst-case dependence on \(H\) (and preferably removes the extra \(\sqrt S\) factor in the leading term), narrowing the gap to the lower bound \(\Omega(H\sqrt{SAK})\) up to logarithmic factors.

Azar et al. It is an problem how to extend their idea and technique to our setting in order to improve the dependancy of the upper bound on H."
